topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared folder opened on Sarah’s screen, displaying a photo of a slightly muddy lake with brightly colored canoes. Her breath hitched, a tiny, sharp sound in the otherwise quiet office. Across the desk, Mark leaned in, his eyes wide, a slow, almost bewildered smile spreading across his face. "Wait a minute," he murmured, tapping the screen. "Is that... Camp Whispering Pines?" Sarah felt a sudden tightness grip her chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. She quickly minimized the window, her fingers hovering over the mouse, wanting desperately to disappear into her keyboard. "I... I think so," she managed, her voice coming out thin and reedy. She focused intently on straightening a stack of papers, the movement jerky and too fast. The silence between them stretched, heavy with unspoken recognition.

[story 2]

Liam stared at the email notification, his knuckles turning white as he gripped his mug. The subject line—'Summer Camp Reunion Photos'—made his stomach clench. He glanced over at Chloe, who was meticulously organizing her desk supplies, her movements unnaturally precise. When she finally looked up, her gaze met his, and a flicker of something unreadable crossed her features. "You know," Chloe began, her tone carefully level, though a slight tremor vibrated in her voice, "I actually went to Whispering Pines last July." Liam swallowed hard, his throat suddenly dry. He shifted in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. "Really?" he asked, the sound barely above a whisper. He found himself tracing the rim of his coffee cup with his thumb, a repetitive, anxious motion.

[story 3]

Maya kept glancing at the calendar on her monitor, a bead of sweat tracing a cold path down her temple. When Ben finally spoke, his voice pitched low, he didn't look directly at her, instead focusing on the beige wall behind her head. "I had a summer at Whispering Pines. Two years ago." Maya felt a sudden urge to stand up, to walk away from the desk and perhaps the entire office. Her hands, resting on the keyboard, began to shake almost imperceptibly. "Oh," she replied, the single syllable sounding fragile. She deliberately turned her chair around, facing the window, trying to anchor herself in the familiar view of the city skyline, wishing fiercely that the floor would simply open up and swallow her whole.
